questions.stem: An integral equation is best described as an equation in which the unknown function appears
questions.explanation: An integral equation is one in which the unknown function $\phi(x)$ appears under one or more integral signs, for example $\phi(x) = f(x) + \lambda\int_a^b K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt$. This contrasts with a differential equation, where the unknown appears through its derivatives. The defining feature is the presence of the unknown inside the integral, so the second choice is correct.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: In the equation $\phi(x) = f(x) + \lambda\int_a^b K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt$, the function $K(x,t)$ is called the
questions.explanation: The function $K(x,t)$ that multiplies the unknown $\phi(t)$ inside the integral is called the kernel of the integral equation. The term $f(x)$ outside the integral is the free term or data function, while $\lambda$ is a parameter. The resolvent and eigenfunctions are objects derived later in the solution process, not the given $K(x,t)$ itself.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: What distinguishes a Fredholm integral equation from a Volterra integral equation?
options: the limits of integration are both constant in Fredholm, while the upper limit is variable in Volterra
Volterra equations have no free term
questions.explanation: The classification into Fredholm versus Volterra rests entirely on the limits of integration. A Fredholm equation has both limits fixed constants, $\int_a^b$. A Volterra equation has a variable upper limit equal to the independent variable, $\int_a^x$. Linearity, the presence of a kernel, and the free term are separate matters that apply to both families.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Classify the equation $\phi(x) = x + \dfrac{1}{2}\int_0^1 (x - t)\phi(t)\,dt$.
options: Volterra equation of the first kind
Volterra equation of the second kind
Fredholm equation of the second kind
Fredholm equation of the first kind
questions.explanation: Both limits of integration are constants, $0$ and $1$, so the equation is of Fredholm type. The unknown $\phi(x)$ appears both outside the integral, as the left side, and inside it, so it is of the second kind. Together this makes it a Fredholm equation of the second kind. The fixed limits rule out Volterra, and the unknown appearing outside rules out the first kind.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Classify the equation $\phi(x) = 1 + \int_0^x (x - t)\,\phi(t)\,dt$.
options: Fredholm equation of the first kind
Volterra equation of the first kind
Volterra equation of the second kind
Fredholm equation of the second kind
questions.explanation: The upper limit of integration is the variable $x$, so the equation is of Volterra type. The unknown $\phi$ appears both outside the integral, as the term on the left, and inside it, so it is of the second kind. Hence it is a Volterra equation of the second kind. The variable upper limit excludes Fredholm, and the appearance of $\phi$ outside excludes the first kind.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: An integral equation of the first kind is one in which the unknown function appears
options: both inside and outside the integral
questions.explanation: In an equation of the first kind the unknown $\phi$ occurs only under the integral sign, as in $f(x) = \int_a^b K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt$, with no copy of $\phi(x)$ standing alone outside. When the unknown also appears outside the integral the equation is of the second kind. The unknown is never the kernel, which is the given function $K(x,t)$.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Classify the equation $\sin x = \int_0^1 e^{xt}\,\phi(t)\,dt$.
options: Volterra equation of the second kind
Fredholm equation of the second kind
Fredholm equation of the first kind
Volterra equation of the first kind
questions.explanation: Both limits, $0$ and $1$, are constants, so the equation is of Fredholm type. The unknown $\phi$ appears only under the integral sign, with no free $\phi(x)$ on the left, where instead we have the given $\sin x$. So it is of the first kind. Together this is a Fredholm equation of the first kind. The constant limits exclude Volterra.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Classify the equation $x^2 = \int_0^x \cos(x - t)\,\phi(t)\,dt$.
options: Fredholm equation of the second kind
Volterra equation of the first kind
Fredholm equation of the first kind
Volterra equation of the second kind
questions.explanation: The upper limit of integration is the variable $x$, so the equation is of Volterra type. The unknown $\phi$ appears only inside the integral, with the left side being the given function $x^2$ and no free $\phi(x)$ outside. So it is of the first kind. Hence it is a Volterra equation of the first kind.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: A homogeneous integral equation is one in which
options: the limits of integration are equal
the free term $f(x)$ is identically zero
questions.explanation: An integral equation $\phi(x) = f(x) + \lambda\int_a^b K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt$ is called homogeneous when the free term satisfies $f(x) \equiv 0$, leaving $\phi(x) = \lambda\int_a^b K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt$. Symmetry of the kernel is a separate property. Setting $\lambda = 0$ would force $\phi = f$ trivially, and equal limits would collapse the integral, so neither defines homogeneity.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Which equation is nonlinear in the unknown function $\phi$?
questions.explanation: An integral equation is linear when the unknown $\phi$ enters only to the first power inside the integral. In the fourth equation the integrand contains $\phi(t)^2$, a square of the unknown, which makes the equation nonlinear. The other three have $\phi(t)$ appearing to the first power, multiplied only by given functions of $x$ and $t$, so they are linear.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: The standard form of a Volterra integral equation of the second kind is
questions.explanation: A Volterra equation has a variable upper limit $x$, and second kind means the unknown appears both outside and inside the integral. Combining these gives $\phi(x) = f(x) + \lambda\int_a^x K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt$. The form with constant upper limit $b$ is Fredholm, and the forms with no free $\phi(x)$ on the left are first kind.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: A kernel of the form $K(x,t) = 1 + xt + x^2 t^2$ is called
questions.explanation: A kernel is separable, also called degenerate, when it can be written as a finite sum of products of a function of $x$ times a function of $t$, $K(x,t) = \sum_i g_i(x)h_i(t)$. Here $1 + xt + x^2t^2 = (1)(1) + (x)(t) + (x^2)(t^2)$, a sum of three such products, so it is separable. This structure is what reduces a Fredholm equation to an algebraic system.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: A kernel that depends on its arguments only through the difference $x - t$, that is $K(x,t) = k(x - t)$, is called a
questions.explanation: When a kernel has the form $K(x,t) = k(x - t)$, depending only on the difference of its arguments, it is called a difference kernel, and the resulting integral is a convolution. Such kernels appear naturally in Volterra equations and make Laplace transform methods effective. A degenerate kernel is a sum of separated products, and a symmetric kernel satisfies $K(x,t) = K(t,x)$, which are different conditions.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Which kernel is symmetric, that is satisfies $K(x,t) = K(t,x)$?
questions.explanation: A kernel is symmetric when swapping $x$ and $t$ leaves it unchanged. For $K(x,t) = x^2 t^2$, interchanging gives $t^2 x^2 = x^2 t^2$, so it is symmetric. For $x - t$ the swap gives $t - x$, the negative, so it is antisymmetric. For $x e^t$ the swap gives $t e^x$, and for $x + 2t$ the swap gives $t + 2x$, neither equal to the original.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: An equation in which the unknown function appears both under an integral sign and through its derivatives is called
options: a Fredholm equation of the first kind
questions.explanation: When derivatives of the unknown $\phi$ appear alongside an integral of $\phi$, as in $\phi'(x) = f(x) + \lambda\int_a^x K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt$, the equation is called an integro-differential equation. A singular equation instead has an infinite limit or an unbounded kernel. The presence of a derivative is the distinguishing feature here.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Why is the equation $\phi(x) = 1 + \int_0^\infty e^{-xt}\,\phi(t)\,dt$ classified as singular?
options: because one limit of integration is infinite
because the unknown appears outside the integral
questions.explanation: An integral equation is singular when a limit of integration is infinite or when the kernel becomes unbounded within the interval. Here the upper limit is $\infty$, which makes the equation singular. A symmetric kernel, a constant free term, and the unknown appearing outside the integral are ordinary features that do not, by themselves, make an equation singular.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: In the Abel equation $f(x) = \displaystyle\int_0^x \dfrac{\phi(t)}{\sqrt{x - t}}\,dt$, which features are present?
options: Fredholm type, first kind, and symmetric
Volterra type, first kind, and singular
questions.explanation: The upper limit is the variable $x$, so the equation is Volterra type. The unknown $\phi$ appears only under the integral, so it is of the first kind. The kernel $1/\sqrt{x - t}$ becomes unbounded as $t \to x$, so the equation is also singular. All three descriptions, Volterra, first kind, and singular, apply, which is exactly the Abel equation.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Classify $\phi(x) = x^3 + \dfrac{1}{6}\displaystyle\int_{-1}^{1} (xt + 1)\,\phi(t)\,dt$ as fully as possible.
options: homogeneous Volterra equation of the first kind
nonlinear Fredholm equation of the second kind
linear Volterra equation of the second kind with a difference kernel
linear Fredholm equation of the second kind with a separable kernel
questions.explanation: The limits $-1$ and $1$ are constants, so the equation is Fredholm. The unknown appears outside and inside, so it is second kind. The integrand has $\phi(t)$ to the first power, so it is linear. The kernel $xt + 1 = (x)(t) + (1)(1)$ is a sum of products of functions of $x$ and $t$, so it is separable. The free term $x^3$ is nonzero, so it is not homogeneous.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Reading the equation $g(x) = \displaystyle\int_a^b K(x,t)\,\phi(t)\,dt$, which statement is correct?
options: $K$ is the unknown to be solved for
$\phi$ is known and $g$ is the unknown to be found
the equation has no solution because $\phi$ appears once
$\phi$ is the unknown, sought from the given data $g$ and kernel $K$
questions.explanation: In an integral equation the function under the integral, $\phi$, is the unknown to be determined, while the data function $g(x)$ and the kernel $K(x,t)$ are given. Here $\phi$ appearing only inside the integral marks the equation as a first kind problem, where we recover $\phi$ from $g$ and $K$. The kernel is given, not solved for, and appearing once does not prevent a solution.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: The parameter $\lambda$ in $\phi(x) = f(x) + \lambda\int_a^b K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt$ is most accurately described as
options: the free term of the equation
a scalar parameter multiplying the integral term
questions.explanation: The symbol $\lambda$ is a scalar parameter that multiplies the entire integral term. Its value is central to the theory: certain special values, the characteristic values or eigenvalues, allow nontrivial solutions of the homogeneous equation. The kernel is $K(x,t)$, the free term is $f(x)$, and the upper limit is $b$, so $\lambda$ is none of those.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Classify $\phi(x) = e^x + \displaystyle\int_0^x \sin(x - t)\,\phi(t)\,dt$ by type, kind, and kernel structure.
questions.explanation: The upper limit is the variable $x$, so the equation is Volterra. The unknown appears outside, as $\phi(x)$, and inside, so it is second kind. The kernel $\sin(x - t)$ depends only on the difference $x - t$, so it is a difference (convolution type) kernel. This combination of Volterra, second kind, and difference kernel is what makes Laplace transforms a natural solution tool.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Which of the following is a homogeneous Fredholm integral equation of the second kind?
questions.explanation: Homogeneous means the free term is zero, second kind means $\phi$ appears outside the integral, and Fredholm means both limits are constant. The second option, $\phi(x) = \lambda\int_0^1 xt\,\phi(t)\,dt$, has no free term, has $\phi(x)$ on the left, and has fixed limits $0$ and $1$, satisfying all three. The first has a nonzero free term $x$, the third is first kind, and the fourth is Volterra.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Consider the equation $\phi(x) = x^2 + \dfrac{1}{3}\displaystyle\int_0^1 (x + t)\,\phi(t)\,dt$.
questions.solution: Because both limits of integration are constants, the equation is of {{1}} type. Since the unknown $\phi$ appears both outside and inside the integral, it is of the {{2}} kind. The kernel $K(x,t) = x + t$ is separable, written as a sum of {{3}} products of a function of $x$ and a function of $t$.
questions.explanation: Constant limits $0$ and $1$ make the equation Fredholm. The unknown appearing both outside the integral and inside makes it second kind. The kernel $x + t = (x)(1) + (1)(t)$ splits into $2$ products, each a function of $x$ times a function of $t$, so it is separable with two terms.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Consider the equation $f(x) = \displaystyle\int_0^x (x - t)\,\phi(t)\,dt$.
questions.solution: The upper limit of integration is the variable $x$, so the equation is of {{1}} type. The unknown $\phi$ appears only under the integral sign, so the equation is of the {{2}} kind. The kernel depends only on the difference of its arguments, so it is a {{3}} kernel.
questions.explanation: The variable upper limit $x$ makes the equation Volterra. The unknown appearing only inside the integral, with $f(x)$ as the given left side, makes it first kind. The kernel $x - t$ depends only on the difference $x - t$, so it is a difference kernel, also called convolution type.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: A general linear integral equation is written $\phi(x) = f(x) + \lambda\displaystyle\int K(x,t)\,\phi(t)\,dt$.
questions.solution: The given function $f(x)$ standing outside the integral is called the {{1}} term. When this term is identically zero, the equation is said to be {{2}}. The function $K(x,t)$ multiplying the unknown inside the integral is called the {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The term $f(x)$ outside the integral is the free term, sometimes called the forcing or data term. When $f(x) \equiv 0$ the equation reduces to $\phi(x) = \lambda\int K\phi\,dt$ and is called homogeneous. The function $K(x,t)$ inside the integral is the kernel.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Consider the equation $\phi(x) = 1 + \displaystyle\int_0^x [\phi(t)]^2\,dt$.
questions.solution: The upper limit is the variable $x$, so the equation is of {{1}} type. Because the integrand contains the square of the unknown, the equation is {{2}} rather than linear. The highest power to which $\phi$ is raised inside the integral is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The variable upper limit $x$ makes the equation Volterra. The integrand $[\phi(t)]^2$ involves the unknown to the second power, so the equation is nonlinear. The highest power of $\phi$ appearing is $2$, which is precisely what breaks linearity.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Consider the equation $\sin x = \displaystyle\int_0^1 x t\,\phi(t)\,dt$.
questions.solution: Both limits are constants, so the equation is of {{1}} type. The unknown appears only under the integral, so it is of the {{2}} kind. The kernel $K(x,t) = xt$ is separable as a single product $g(x)h(t)$ with $g(x) =$ {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Constant limits $0$ and $1$ make the equation Fredholm. The unknown appearing only inside, with $\sin x$ as the given data, makes it first kind. The kernel $xt$ factors as $g(x)h(t)$ with $g(x) = x$ and $h(t) = t$, a single separable product.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations

questions.stem: Consider the equation $\phi(x) = e^x + \displaystyle\int_0^1 e^{x+t}\,\phi(t)\,dt$.
questions.solution: The equation is Fredholm of the {{1}} kind. The kernel $K(x,t) = e^{x+t}$ can be factored using a law of exponents as $e^x e^{t}$, showing it is separable with the number of terms in the sum equal to {{2}}. Swapping $x$ and $t$ leaves $e^{x+t}$ unchanged, so the kernel is also {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The unknown appears both outside and inside with constant limits, so the equation is Fredholm of the second kind. By $e^{x+t} = e^x e^t$ the kernel is a single product $g(x)h(t)$, so the separable sum has $1$ term. Interchanging $x$ and $t$ gives $e^{t+x} = e^{x+t}$, so the kernel is symmetric.
questions.source: in the style of Wazwaz, A First Course in Integral Equations
